---
title: ""
page-layout: full
toc: false
---

<style>
#quarto-content { max-width: none !important; padding: 0 !important; margin: 0 !important; }
main.content, #quarto-document-content { padding: 0 !important; }
.quarto-title-block { display: none !important; }
body { overflow-x: hidden; }
.sr { opacity: 0; transform: translateY(36px); transition: opacity 0.75s ease, transform 0.75s ease; }
.sr.from-left { transform: translateX(-44px); }
.sr.scale-in { transform: scale(0.95) translateY(12px); }
.sr.visible { opacity: 1; transform: none; }
.sr.d1 { transition-delay: 0.1s; }
.sr.d2 { transition-delay: 0.2s; }
.sr.d3 { transition-delay: 0.3s; }
.o-hero {
  min-height: 94vh;
  background: linear-gradient(155deg, #0a1f1e 0%, #122d2a 55%, #0d3b38 100%);
  display: flex;
  align-items: center;
  justify-content: center;
  padding: 6rem 2rem;
  text-align: center;
  position: relative;
  overflow: hidden;
}
.o-hero::before {
  content: '';
  position: absolute;
  inset: 0;
  background: radial-gradient(ellipse at 60% 30%, rgba(42,157,143,.18) 0%, transparent 65%);
}
.o-hero-inner { max-width: 820px; position: relative; z-index: 1; }
.o-hero-label { font-size: .78rem; letter-spacing: .22em; text-transform: uppercase; color: #2a9d8f; font-weight: 700; margin-bottom: 1.6rem; }
.o-hero h1 { font-size: clamp(2.6rem, 7vw, 5.2rem); font-weight: 800; line-height: 1.08; color: #fff; margin-bottom: 1.6rem; letter-spacing: -.025em; }
.o-hero h1 em { color: #2a9d8f; font-style: normal; }
.o-hero-sub { font-size: 1.15rem; color: rgba(255,255,255,.65); max-width: 540px; margin: 0 auto 3rem; line-height: 1.75; }
.o-hero-scroll { display: inline-flex; flex-direction: column; align-items: center; gap: .55rem; font-size: .78rem; letter-spacing: .16em; text-transform: uppercase; color: rgba(255,255,255,.28); }
.o-hero-scroll::after { content: ''; width: 1px; height: 52px; background: linear-gradient(to bottom, rgba(42,157,143,.7), transparent); animation: scroll-pulse 2.2s ease-in-out infinite; }
@keyframes scroll-pulse { 0%,100% { opacity: .3; transform: scaleY(1); } 50% { opacity: 1; transform: scaleY(1.12); } }
.o-intro { background: #f7faf9; padding: 5.5rem 2rem; text-align: center; border-bottom: 1px solid #e4efed; }
.o-intro-inner { max-width: 680px; margin: 0 auto; }
.o-intro p { font-size: 1.18rem; line-height: 1.82; color: #3a3a3a; margin: 0; }
.o-intro strong { color: #2a9d8f; }
.o-chapter { padding: 7rem 2rem; position: relative; }
.o-chapter:nth-child(odd) { background: #fff; }
.o-chapter:nth-child(even) { background: #f7faf9; }
.o-chapter-inner { max-width: 920px; margin: 0 auto; }
.o-ch-tag { display: inline-flex; align-items: center; gap: .6rem; font-size: .72rem; letter-spacing: .22em; text-transform: uppercase; color: #2a9d8f; font-weight: 700; margin-bottom: .9rem; }
.o-ch-tag::before { content: ''; display: inline-block; width: 22px; height: 2px; background: #2a9d8f; border-radius: 2px; }
.o-ch-header h2 { font-size: clamp(1.9rem, 4.2vw, 3rem); font-weight: 800; color: #111; line-height: 1.13; letter-spacing: -.025em; margin-bottom: .6rem; }
.o-ch-sub { font-size: .88rem; color: #999; text-transform: uppercase; letter-spacing: .08em; font-weight: 500; }
.o-ch-divider { width: 52px; height: 3px; background: #2a9d8f; border-radius: 2px; margin: 1.8rem 0 2.6rem; }
.o-insight { background: #0a1f1e; border-radius: 14px; padding: 2.4rem 3rem; margin: 2rem 0 2.6rem; position: relative; overflow: hidden; }
.o-insight::before { content: '\201C'; position: absolute; top: -.15em; left: .25em; font-size: 14rem; color: rgba(42,157,143,.07); font-family: Georgia, serif; line-height: 1; pointer-events: none; }
.o-insight p { font-size: 1.22rem; color: #fff; line-height: 1.68; font-style: italic; margin: 0; position: relative; z-index: 1; }
.o-insight p strong { color: #2a9d8f; font-style: normal; }
.o-ch-body { margin: 0 0 2rem; }
.o-ch-body p { font-size: 1.04rem; line-height: 1.82; color: #444; margin: 0 0 1rem; }
.o-ch-body p:last-child { margin-bottom: 0; }
.o-stats { display: flex; gap: 1.4rem; margin-top: 3rem; flex-wrap: wrap; }
.o-stat { flex: 1; min-width: 130px; background: #fff; border: 1px solid #e0ebe9; border-radius: 12px; padding: 1.4rem 1.2rem; text-align: center; box-shadow: 0 2px 10px rgba(0,0,0,.045); }
.o-chapter:nth-child(odd) .o-stat { background: #f7faf9; }
.o-stat-num { display: block; font-size: 2.2rem; font-weight: 800; color: #2a9d8f; line-height: 1; margin-bottom: .45rem; letter-spacing: -.02em; }
.o-stat-label { font-size: .82rem; color: #777; line-height: 1.4; }
.o-closing { background: linear-gradient(150deg, #0a1f1e 0%, #0d3b38 100%); padding: 7.5rem 2rem; text-align: center; }
.o-closing-inner { max-width: 700px; margin: 0 auto; }
.o-closing-tag { font-size: .74rem; letter-spacing: .22em; text-transform: uppercase; color: #2a9d8f; font-weight: 700; display: block; margin-bottom: 1.4rem; }
.o-closing h2 { font-size: clamp(2rem, 4.5vw, 3.2rem); font-weight: 800; color: #fff; line-height: 1.15; letter-spacing: -.025em; margin-bottom: 1.6rem; }
.o-closing p { font-size: 1.1rem; color: rgba(255,255,255,.65); line-height: 1.8; margin: 0; }
.o-closing strong { color: rgba(255,255,255,.9); }
@media (max-width: 640px) {
  .o-insight { padding: 1.6rem 1.4rem; }
  .o-chapter { padding: 4.5rem 1.4rem; }
  .o-stats { gap: .9rem; }
  .o-stat-num { font-size: 1.8rem; }
}
</style>

<section class="o-hero">
<div class="o-hero-inner sr">
<p class="o-hero-label"></p>
<h1>Markets, <em>Conspirancy</em> &amp; Enforcement</h1>
<p class="o-hero-sub">Why competition fails? How firms interact? Whay happens if we intervene the market? </p>
<div class="o-hero-scroll">Scroll to explore</div>
</div>
</section>

<section class="o-intro">
<div class="o-intro-inner sr">
<p>I'm an economist who studies <strong>how companies behave when they're supposed to compete, and what happens when they don't</strong> . My research focuses on two industries that affect everyone: electricity and construction. Here is what I've found.</p>
</div>
</section>

<section class="o-chapter">
<div class="o-chapter-inner">
<div class="o-ch-header sr from-left">
<p class="o-ch-tag">Chapter 01  Energy Markets</p>
<h2>Can AI Catch Cheaters<br>in Electricity Markets?</h2>
<p class="o-ch-sub">With Martin Huber &amp; Jeremy Proz</p>
</div>
<div class="o-ch-divider sr"></div>
<div class="o-insight sr scale-in">
<p>A machine learning algorithm can detect secret price-fixing in energy markets <strong>faster and more reliably</strong> than traditional investigation, by spotting patterns that are invisible to the human eye.</p>
</div>
<div class="o-ch-body sr">
<p><strong>The problem.</strong> In electricity markets, energy companies sometimes secretly coordinate their bids  or deliberately hold back power they could provide  to push prices up. These cartels cost consumers and businesses billions, but they're notoriously hard to catch. Traditional investigations are slow, reactive, and expensive.</p>
<p><strong>What we did.</strong> We applied machine learning  the same technology that filters your spam and flags suspicious credit card transactions to bidding records from the Italian electricity market. The algorithm scans thousands of bids and automatically flags suspicious patterns that suggest collusion, far faster than any human analyst could.</p>
<p><strong>The novelty.</strong> We also developed novel signals to detect <em>capacity withholding</em>: when producers deliberately offer less power than they actually have available, creating artificial scarcity to drive up prices. These signals come from the day-ahead market, where data is more transparent and timely.</p>
<p><strong>The finding.</strong> The algorithm successfully identified known cartel behavior in both test cases. This is a proof of concept: automated, data-driven surveillance could fundamentally change how regulators monitor energy markets  catching cheaters earlier and at far lower cost.</p>
</div>
<div class="o-stats">
<div class="o-stat sr d1">
<span class="o-stat-num">2</span>
<span class="o-stat-label">Confirmed cartel<br>cases tested</span>
</div>
<div class="o-stat sr d2">
<span class="o-stat-num">ML</span>
<span class="o-stat-label">Supervised learning<br>for cartel detection</span>
</div>
<div class="o-stat sr d3">
<span class="o-stat-num">Italy</span>
<span class="o-stat-label">Wholesale electricity<br>market studied</span>
</div>
</div>
</div>
</section>

<section class="o-chapter">
<div class="o-chapter-inner">
<div class="o-ch-header sr from-left">
<p class="o-ch-tag">Chapter 02  Market Geography</p>
<h2>The Hidden Map<br>of Competition</h2>
<p class="o-ch-sub">With Julie LeGallo &amp; Jean Dub&eacute;</p>
</div>
<div class="o-ch-divider sr"></div>
<div class="o-insight sr scale-in">
<p>Firms don't compete along official administrative borders. Real competition follows its own invisible geography  and mapping it reveals <strong>47 natural markets that look nothing like the official map.</strong></p>
</div>
<div class="o-ch-body sr">
<p><strong>The problem.</strong> When regulators decide whether a market is competitive, they need to draw boundaries around it. Typically they use official regions  cities, counties, provinces. But do companies actually compete across those lines? Or do they have their own invisible territories shaped by distance, roads, and local knowledge?</p>
<p><strong>What we did.</strong> Using eight years of bid data from Quebec's municipal road paving contracts, I built a network showing which companies competed with each other based on where they actually chose to bid. I then applied tools borrowed from social network analysis to find natural clusters: groups of firms genuinely fighting over the same turf.</p>
<p><strong>The finding.</strong> We identified <strong>47 distinct competitive areas</strong> that don't match Quebec's administrative map at all. Distance matters enormously: double the distance between two firms and you roughly halve the chance they'll bid on the same project. Competition is intensely local  but locally it follows roads and economic geography, not political lines.</p>
<p><strong>Why it matters.</strong> Regulators using official boundaries may be misjudging market concentration: missing local monopolies in some areas while overestimating competition in others. Better market maps lead to better antitrust policy.</p>
</div>
<div class="o-stats">
<div class="o-stat sr d1">
<span class="o-stat-num">47</span>
<span class="o-stat-label">Functional market<br>areas identified</span>
</div>
<div class="o-stat sr d2">
<span class="o-stat-num">8 yrs</span>
<span class="o-stat-label">Of bidding data<br>from Qu&eacute;bec</span>
</div>
<div class="o-stat sr d3">
<span class="o-stat-num">&minus;1</span>
<span class="o-stat-label">Distance elasticity<br>of competition</span>
</div>
</div>
</div>
</section>

<section class="o-chapter">
<div class="o-chapter-inner">
<div class="o-ch-header sr from-left">
<p class="o-ch-tag">Chapter 03  Antitrust Policy</p>
<h2>Did Intervention Work <br>or do they Keep Business as Usual?</h2>
<p class="o-ch-sub">Job Market Paper</p>
</div>
<div class="o-ch-divider sr"></div>
<div class="o-insight sr scale-in">
<p>Antitrust intervention changed bidding behavior in Qu&eacute;bec's road construction sector  but the effects were <strong>temporary, not permanent.</strong> Breaking a cartel matters, but may not be enough on its own.</p>
</div>
<div class="o-ch-body sr">
<p><strong>The problem.</strong> For years, road construction companies in Qu&eacute;bec secretly agreed on who would win which contract  a classic cartel that cost taxpayers hundreds of millions of dollars. When authorities launched an antitrust inquiry and dismantled the cartel, a key question remained: did that actually make the market more competitive in the long run?</p>
<p><strong>What I did.</strong> I tracked bidding behavior across hundreds of Qu&eacute;bec municipalities from 2011 to 2022  before, during, and after the antitrust intervention. To isolate the true effect of the crackdown from everything else happening in the economy at the same time, I used a statistical method that makes treated and untreated municipalities more comparable before comparing them.</p>
<p><strong>The finding.</strong> Yes, behavior changed after the intervention  bids became more competitive initially. But those effects were <strong>temporary rather than lasting</strong>. Over time, the market settled back toward patterns that resembled the pre-intervention era. The cartel was broken, but the underlying structural features of the market  its geographic fragmentation, high barriers to entry, and small number of local players  remained.</p>
<p><strong>The lesson.</strong> One-time enforcement actions, however powerful, may not produce lasting competitive change. Sustained oversight  combined with structural reforms that lower barriers to entry  may be needed to keep markets genuinely competitive after a cartel is dissolved.</p>
</div>
<div class="o-stats">
<div class="o-stat sr d1">
<span class="o-stat-num">12 yrs</span>
<span class="o-stat-label">Of data tracked<br>2011 &ndash; 2022</span>
</div>
<div class="o-stat sr d2">
<span class="o-stat-num">DiD</span>
<span class="o-stat-label">Difference-in-differences<br>with entropy balance</span>
</div>
<div class="o-stat sr d3">
<span class="o-stat-num">?</span>
<span class="o-stat-label">Long-run effects<br>still uncertain</span>
</div>
</div>
</div>
</section>

<section class="o-closing">
<div class="o-closing-inner sr">
<span class="o-closing-tag">Why This Research Matters</span>
<h2>Markets Are Not Neutral</h2>
<p>Competition in markets affects all of us  through electricity bills, the quality of our roads, and how public money gets spent. <strong>When companies cheat, we all pay.</strong> My research shows that these problems can be detected with new tools, that enforcement can help, and that we still have much to learn about making markets work for everyone.</p>
</div>
</section>

<script>
(function () {
  var io = new IntersectionObserver(function (entries) {
    entries.forEach(function (entry) {
      if (entry.isIntersecting) {
        entry.target.classList.add('visible');
        io.unobserve(entry.target);
      }
    });
  }, { threshold: 0.13 });
  document.querySelectorAll('.sr').forEach(function (el) { io.observe(el); });
})();
</script>
